title: The Alt-Phillips Functional for Negative Powers
speaker: Daniela De Silva
abstract: We discuss a one-phase degenerate free boundary problem which arises from the minimization of the so-called Alt-Phillips functional. We establish partial regularity results for the free boundary and discuss the rigidity of global minimizers when gamma approaches the endpoints of the interval of admissible values.

proof_stages.label: Variational potentials and perimeter limits
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from the standard variational principle that minimizers concentrate near zeros of a potential, recalls the Allen-Cahn/Modica-Mortola picture, and explains that nonsmooth behavior of the potential at its minimum leads naturally to free boundaries.

proof_stages.label: Classical one-phase free-boundary paradigm
proof_stages.summary: Obstacle and Bernoulli/Alt-Caffarelli problems are used to set the benchmark questions of existence, optimal regularity, blow-up analysis, monotonicity formulas, classification of homogeneous global solutions, and resulting free-boundary regularity or singularity.

proof_stages.label: Positive Alt-Phillips interpolation
proof_stages.summary: The positive-power Alt-Phillips potential is presented as interpolating between Bernoulli and obstacle-type behavior, with known optimal regularity, density estimates, finite perimeter of the free boundary, and smoothness of the reduced free boundary.

proof_stages.label: Negative-power functional and degeneracy
proof_stages.summary: The new problem is introduced for singular negative powers in the range corresponding to exponents between 0 and -2, and a one-dimensional Euler-Lagrange/asymptotic analysis shows that the free-boundary condition is encoded in a higher-order term while the gradient blows up at the boundary.

proof_stages.label: Viscosity formulation, calibration, and blow-up compactness
proof_stages.summary: Because direct integration-by-parts free-boundary conditions are ineffective in the degenerate regime, the argument proves that minimizers are viscosity solutions via calibrations and establishes the core estimates: Hölder regularity, nondegeneracy, Weiss-type monotonicity, and existence of homogeneous blow-ups.

proof_stages.label: Cone classification and flat-implies-regularity theory
proof_stages.summary: For fixed negative exponent, the classification of minimizing cones is available in dimension two, and together with an improvement-of-flatness theorem it yields C^{1,β} free-boundary regularity away from a singular set of controlled dimension.

proof_stages.label: Gamma-convergence and compactness near the singular endpoint
proof_stages.summary: As the negative exponent approaches the endpoint, suitable rescalings of the functional first give a perimeter Gamma-limit and then a compact Dirichlet-plus-perimeter limit, with uniform estimates needed to pass minimizers and regularity information to the limit.

proof_stages.label: Transfer of minimal-surface regularity to singular-set bounds
proof_stages.summary: The limiting minimal-surface theory and the uniform flat-implies-regularity machinery yield smoothness in low dimensions near the endpoint and lower bounds on the first possible dimension of singular cones, with analogous endpoint information near the Alt-Caffarelli limit.

examples.name: Allen-Cahn double-well transition layer
examples.note: The double-well potential and its one-dimensional transition profile make concrete the general fact that variational potentials can force phase separation and perimeter-type limits, but the later negative-power argument does not use this particular model.
examples.engagement_reason: she analyzes the Allen-Cahn double-well by rescaling minimizers, describing gamma convergence to perimeter, and doing a one-dimensional Euler-Lagrange/transition-layer computation

examples.name: Two-dimensional Bernoulli/minimal-surface correspondence
examples.note: The known correspondence between planar Bernoulli critical points and immersed minimal surfaces with a symmetry plane illustrates an older, dimension-specific link between Bernoulli-type free boundaries and minimal surfaces.

examples.name: One-dimensional threshold profile near exponent minus two
examples.note: The one-dimensional profile with square behaving like an unintegrable inverse power is used to explain why, near the singular endpoint, most energy concentrates near the free boundary and a perimeter limit should emerge.
